overview.executiveSummary: Landmark week. Stefan won 1st place at the HSIL Harvard Health Hackathon at KIIT University, Bhubaneswar with project NORA — progressing to Harvard boot camp. Turned 25 on April 11. OMI captured 149 conversations (81,772 words) — the richest data week ever. The hackathon dominated the week (69+ conversations), producing deep signal on healthcare startup mentoring, VR medical education, AI nutrition platforms, and infection control innovation. Critical next moves: (1) Lock NORA follow-up deliverables for Harvard selection round — letters of intent, business plan, prototype plan; (2) Action the team hours expansion for Dalia + Jennifer before verbal commitments decay further; (3) Submit ADA block content by Apr 14 deadline; (4) Prepare for USCIS biometrics Apr 23 in San Fernando; (5) Plan return flight BBI → HYD → SFO around Apr 24-28. The week also surfaced 6+ startup concepts from mentoring sessions, VR curriculum design breakthroughs, and concrete grant budgeting insights.

crossSourceAlignment.statusLabel: OMI only — needs Airtable epic + Notion doc ASAP
OMI only — strongest signal, zero documentation
OMI confirms active — needs Airtable sync
Biometrics Apr 23 — must be back in CA

overview.redFlag: NORA won 1st place but the Harvard boot camp selection requires letters of intent, a basic business plan, and prototype progress within weeks. This momentum will evaporate without immediate structured follow-through. Meanwhile, the ADA block submission deadline is April 14 (tomorrow) — Stephanie must have sent content to Dalia. Also: USCIS biometrics on April 23 in San Fernando means you MUST book return flights NOW.

tasks.title: Book Return Flight — BBI → HYD → SFO
tasks.description: USCIS biometrics appointment April 23 in San Fernando, California. Must book multi-leg return flight from Bhubaneswar through Hyderabad to San Francisco. Target arrival by April 22 to allow buffer. I-765 Employment Authorization receipt: IOE9361516198.
checklist: Search flights BBI → HYD → SFO for Apr 20-22 arrival
Book flight (consider layover time at HYD)
Update USCIS address to California address before appointment
Confirm San Fernando biometrics location and appointment time
Set reminder for day before with documents checklist
tasks.claudePrompt:
I need to book a return flight from Bhubaneswar (BBI), India to San Francisco (SFO) via Hyderabad (HYD). I must arrive by April 22, 2026 because I have a USCIS biometrics appointment on April 23 in San Fernando, California.

Context: I'm currently in Bhubaneswar after the HSIL Harvard Health Hackathon. My I-765 Employment Authorization receipt number is IOE9361516198. I also need to update my address to my Stanford/California address before the appointment.

Please help me:
1. Search for optimal flight routes and dates (Apr 20-22 arrival)
2. Draft a checklist of documents I need for the USCIS biometrics appointment
3. Create a pre-travel checklist (passport, visa, I-797 receipt notice, photos)
4. Draft the address change notification if needed

tasks.claudePrompt:
I just won 1st place at the HSIL Harvard Health Hackathon in India with my project NORA. I'm progressing to the Harvard boot camp, but 50% of teams will be cut in the next selection round.

Context: NORA is a healthcare innovation project. Harvard venture judges seek detailed validation before investing. I need to show concrete progress: hospital letters of intent, a basic business plan, and prototype direction. My team currently lacks dedicated engineers. ATBI has offered complimentary incubation.

Please help me:
1. Create a 2-week action plan for boot camp preparation
2. Draft a hospital letter of intent template I can customize
3. Outline a lean business plan structure (1-2 pages)
4. Draft an agenda for our first weekly 1-hr team meeting
5. List the key metrics Harvard judges will evaluate

tasks.claudePrompt:
I manage a research team at Stanford (NOURISH lab) and need to increase hours for two team members. This has been a verbal intention for 3+ weeks and I reviewed the budget with Eli on April 2.

Context:
- Dalia: Currently $16/hour, ~3 hours/week (every Thursday at 12:00). Handles data analysis and ADA block content. Want to increase by ~5 hrs/week.
- Jennifer: Part-time outreach and communication. Want to increase by ~5 hrs/week and formalize delegation.
- Budget: Multiple study grants with breathing space. Personnel include Mehak, Stephanie, Reha/Raham, Christopher, Prince.
- Grant sources: PFME budget, study personnel allocation (~$2,500), potential advance funds.

Please help me:
1. Draft personalized messages to Dalia and Jennifer proposing the increase
2. Draft a budget justification paragraph referencing the Eli meeting
3. Create a weekly schedule tracker for their updated hours
4. Draft the Jennifer delegation SOP (tasks she should fully own: recruitment outreach, community partner comms, weekly status follow-ups)

tasks.title: Jennifer Delegation SOP
tasks.description: Jennifer should own: participant recruitment outreach, community partner communications, and weekly status follow-ups. No SOP or framework exists. Create it this week alongside the hours expansion.
checklist: Define 3-5 tasks Jennifer should fully own
Create one-page delegation SOP
Include decision rights and escalation path
Create outreach email template
Create weekly async check-in template
Schedule 15-min alignment call
tasks.claudePrompt:
I want to delegate more research outreach and communication tasks to Jennifer on my NOURISH research team at Stanford. I've been saying this for 3+ weeks — now I need the actual framework.

Context: Jennifer is part-time and handles some outreach. I want her to fully own: participant recruitment outreach, community partner communications, and weekly status follow-ups. She has good communication skills but needs clear scope and templates. I'm also increasing her hours.

Please help me:
1. Write a 1-page delegation SOP covering her owned tasks, decision rights, and escalation path
2. Create an outreach email template for participant recruitment
3. Create a weekly async check-in template (5-minute format)
4. Draft a short kickoff message I can send her

tasks.claudePrompt:
I need to complete my US tax return filing. I started during a trip but didn't finish.

Context: I'm an Australian citizen working at Stanford University School of Medicine as a Research Tech. I have a W-2 from my California employer (Stanford). I partially filled in: ZIP code, income tax, Social Security wages/withheld, state (California). My home address is Australian but I have a Stanford address at 203 Rosse Lane, Rains Student Housing, Stanford, CA 94305.

Please help me:
1. Determine whether I should use my Australian or California address for filing
2. Identify which tax forms I need as a non-resident alien or resident alien (based on substantial presence test)
3. Create a checklist of documents I need to complete the return
4. Suggest the best free/low-cost filing option for my situation

ideas.pgQuoteTop: Live in the future, then build what's missing.
ideas.pgQuoteBottom: The best startup ideas are the ones that seem like bad ideas to most people.

ideas.title: NORA — Harvard Boot Camp Track
ideas.subtitle: The Hackathon Win That Became a Company
ideas.edge: 1st place win + Harvard pipeline + Stanford credibility
ideas.essay: NORA just won 1st at HSIL. It's no longer an idea — it's a venture in selection. Harvard judges want validation, not vision. The next 4 weeks determine whether this becomes a real startup or joins the hackathon-winner graveyard. Your edge: Stanford affiliation for credibility, Apollo Hospitals as distribution (from NOURISH network), clinical expertise that most tech teams lack. The risk isn't competition — it's attention fragmentation. You're running NOURISH, PFME, CGM, Shoei, VR curriculum, and now NORA. Harvard judges will smell split focus. Dedicate the next 2 weeks to NORA deliverables or consciously defer.

ideas.title: The Cultural Plate
ideas.subtitle: AI Meal Designer That Respects Your Grandmother
ideas.essay: This week's hackathon confirmed the thesis from multiple angles. You mentored a team building AI-driven cost-optimized nutrition recommendations for Indian hospitals — they're building a piece of what you're already architecting. The NIN Hyderabad/ICMR-NIN datasets were explicitly discussed. Your differentiation: TDA (Topological Data Analysis) for flavor molecule networks, not just LLM prompting. Phase 1 is hospital deployment (canteen-less facilities). Phase 2 is B2C subscription + cloud kitchen integration. The mentor critique was sharp: 'Where's your skin in the game? What's the revenue model?' Answer that before Harvard.

ideas.title: Shoei — AI Wearable for Hospital Infection Control
ideas.subtitle: Smart Footwear That Watches the Floor
ideas.edge: Infection control data + hospital ops intelligence
ideas.essay: Explicitly surfaced in two OMI conversations this week. The concept: AI wearable footwear that generates hospital infection control data — traffic heat maps, EVS timing optimization, cleaning verification. The real product isn't the shoe — it's the data layer. Hospitals will pay for infection rate reduction data (even 10% reduction = massive liability savings). Vietnam manufacturing makes hardware affordable. You already requested a pitch deck be built. This is the most differentiated startup idea because nobody else is combining wearables + infection control + hospital operations intelligence.

ideas.title: Sentinel — Neonatal Apnea Monitoring (From Mentoring)
ideas.subtitle: Reducing False Alarms in the NICU
ideas.essay: One of the teams you mentored has something real: AI-based neonatal apnea detection using EEG + respiratory effort + ECG + SpO2 to reduce NICU false alarms. They have a provisional patent, pediatrician collaborators, and a high-fidelity lung simulator. Your mentoring advice was precise: miniaturize hardware, embed sensors in hypoallergenic fabric, partner with incubator manufacturers (GE, Siemens). This isn't your startup — but consider an advisor equity position. The neonatal device market is dominated by few players, and this team has the clinical validation path.

ideas.title: Flavor Roulette
ideas.subtitle: The Molecular Cooking Game
ideas.essay: The hackathon surfaced a 'Duolingo for cooking' concept from a friend conversation — micro-lessons taught by chefs with AI at every layer. Combine this with your FlavorDB molecular data: scoring based on real flavor molecule overlap. Players unlock cultural cuisine packs. Level 2 adds a diabetes constraint. This could be the engagement layer for your research platform AND a standalone consumer product.

ideas.title: Glucose Poker
ideas.subtitle: Bet Your Biology
ideas.essay: A dinner party card game. Players wear CGMs, eat the same meal, bet on who spikes highest. Identical meals produce wildly different responses. The educational payload is enormous but disguised as chaos. Your CGM study gives you the data to design this properly.

ideas.title: The Grandmother Hypothesis of Nutrition Science
ideas.subtitle: When You Run the Numbers, Grandmothers Are Right 70-80% of the Time
ideas.edge: You're literally building the tools to prove this
ideas.essay: This week's hackathon conversations repeatedly surfaced the tension between evidence-based nutrition and cultural food wisdom. What if grandmother knowledge is a distributed clinical trial running across millions of households for centuries? Modern tools (NLP, FlavorDB, CGM) can finally extract the signal. Submit to The Lancet as a perspective piece. Your NOURISH data + FlavorDB + CGM results = the evidence base nobody else has.

ideas.title: The Competence Ratio
ideas.subtitle: Why Healthcare Systems Collapse Exponentially
ideas.edge: NOURISH team dynamics + hackathon team observations
ideas.essay: Your OMI captured the insight: a team of 10 with 8 competent people outperforms a team of 100 with 30 — not by 3x but by 30x. This week you saw it live: some hackathon teams had brilliant concepts but couldn't execute, while others with simpler ideas had relentless execution. The collapse is exponential, not linear. Propose the 'Competence Ratio' as a measurable metric for healthcare workforce planning.

ideas.title: The Nutrition Colonialism Map
ideas.subtitle: Which Cuisines Are Invisible to Science?
ideas.essay: Each country colored by what percentage of its traditional cuisine appears in global nutrition databases. France, Italy, Japan: 80%+. India, Nigeria, Ethiopia: under 5%. Overlay with diabetes prevalence. The correlation is devastating. This week's hackathon in India made this visceral — teams repeatedly identified that Indian dietary data is fragmented across NIN, ICMR, FAO with no unified dataset. Animate as time-lapse. Submit to The Lancet.

ideas.title: The Decision Fossil Record
ideas.subtitle: Never Lose Context Again
ideas.edge: Already halfway there with Optimus + Stefan-Brain
ideas.essay: You're building this. Stefan-Brain + Optimus Command Center = a decision fossil record in progress. This week proved its value: 81,772 words of context that would have been lost without OMI extraction. The missing piece: automatic cross-referencing between decisions made in conversations and their downstream execution in Airtable/Notion.

ideas.title: ASHA Worker Decision Support
ideas.subtitle: AI Triage for Rural India's Health Workers
ideas.edge: Hackathon mentoring insight + government platform integration
ideas.essay: From the hackathon mentoring: an AI decision-support tool for ASHA community health workers (not end patients — legal constraint in India). Structured follow-up questions in local languages, risk scores, alerts to nearest health facility. Integration point: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM/ABHA). India's government healthcare infrastructure is the distribution channel. Low smartphone penetration = voice-first design.

ideas.title: The 20-Minute Thesis
ideas.subtitle: What If Every Research Question Could Be Tested in 20 Minutes?
ideas.edge: OMI daily observations + academic friction experience
ideas.essay: This week you went from idea to 1st place in 72 hours at a hackathon. Meanwhile, your IRB modification has been pending for weeks. The bottleneck in science isn't rigor — it's the absurd overhead between insight and test. Constrain every research question to: 'Can I get a meaningful signal in 20 minutes of data collection?' For 30 days, capture one micro-thesis per day from voice notes, test in 20 minutes, publish results.

dataSources.name: Granola
dataSources.desc: Recurring meeting topics, repeated action items, process descriptions

dataSources.name: Airtable
dataSources.desc: Tasks with high 'Times Delayed', recurring patterns, work stream bottlenecks

dataSources.name: OMI

dataSources.name: Notion
dataSources.desc: Process docs, SOPs, templates, repeated page structures

candidates.name: OMI Commitment Capture → Airtable
candidates.pattern: 149 conversations this week = 8+ unactioned verbal commitments identified. At 81K words, manual review is impossible. Automated capture would prevent 80%+ of commitment decay.

candidates.name: Hackathon/Conference Follow-Up Automation
candidates.pattern: HSIL Hackathon generated 50+ new contacts, 10+ mentored teams, 3 LinkedIn posts planned. Currently all manual follow-up. Automate: extract names → draft messages → schedule sends.

candidates.name: Meeting Action Item Extraction
candidates.pattern: After every meeting, 3-5 action items discussed but only ~40% make it into Airtable.

candidates.name: Grant Deadline Tracker + Application Automation
candidates.pattern: Multiple grant deadlines (USDA May 15, ADA May 30, NIH Jun 5, RWJF Jul 1, CDPH Aug 1) with overlapping application requirements. Pre-fill common sections across applications.

candidates.name: Weekly Status Report Generation
candidates.pattern: Stefan synthesizes weekly updates manually from multiple sources.

candidates.name: CGM Participant Follow-Up Templating
candidates.pattern: CGM participant communications follow the same structure every time.

candidates.name: Notion Decision Record Writer
candidates.pattern: Key decisions happen in meetings but rarely get documented in Notion.

crossSourcePatterns.title: Meeting → Task → Doc Pipeline
crossSourcePatterns.desc: Granola meetings generate action items → become Airtable tasks → need Notion docs. Currently manual at every step.
crossSourcePatterns.flow: Granola → manual entry → Airtable → manual write → Notion

crossSourcePatterns.title: Voice → Task Capture (Critical)
crossSourcePatterns.desc: 149 OMI conversations this week = 100+ potential commitments. 8+ confirmed unactioned. Manual extraction at 81K words is impossible.
crossSourcePatterns.flow: OMI voice → lost → should be → Airtable task

crossSourcePatterns.title: Conference → Follow-Up Pipeline
crossSourcePatterns.desc: HSIL Hackathon generated massive contact/idea backlog. No system to track follow-ups, LinkedIn connections, or mentee progress.

crossSourcePatterns.title: Grant Cycle Loop
crossSourcePatterns.desc: IRB feedback from meetings triggers Notion doc updates, which generate new Airtable tasks. 5 grant deadlines in next 4 months.
crossSourcePatterns.flow: Granola meeting → IRB feedback → Notion update → Airtable task

grants.name: USDA AFRI — Food Systems & Metabolic Health
grants.match: Strong alignment with NOURISH community food access work and dietary behavior change arm. Also aligns with Cultural Cuisine Topology Map project.

grants.name: ADA Innovation Award — Technology-Assisted Nutrition Counseling
grants.match: AI nutrition platform + VR education components map to ADA's tech-assisted diabetes prevention priority.

grants.name: NIH R21 — Dietary Patterns & CGM in Diverse Populations
grants.match: Direct fit: CGM + nutrition intervention in underserved communities aligns with NOURISH core protocol. 60-person pilot RCT data strengthens application.

grants.name: Robert Wood Johnson Foundation — Healthier Diet Initiative
grants.match: Health equity focus matches NOURISH's South Bay community partner recruitment strategy.

grants.name: California Department of Public Health — Chronic Disease Prevention
grants.match: State-level fit for NOURISH's California community cohort; requires local partner co-PI.

grants.name: PCORI — Patient-Centered Outcomes (Target: $600K)
grants.match: Primary target. VR nutrition + CGM + culturally tailored care = core PCORI mandate. 30,000+ participants across program channels. Minal Moharir + Isabela Moise as co-investigators.

grants.name: CHPR Symposium Presentation (May 15)
grants.match: Network: Lucas William Lowe, Kate McKinney, Adrienne, Dr. Prochaska, Josh, Tia, Yasaman, Yetsa. Critical for internal Stanford positioning.
